item.title: US Gasoline Prices Fall Below $4 a Gallon as Iran Strain Eases
item.description: US gasoline prices dipped below $4 a gallon for the first time since March, providing welcome relief to consumers after a historic global supply disruption sent fuel costs soaring for months.

item.title: SNB Keeps Up Franc Intervention Threat With Rate Held at Zero
item.description: The Swiss National Bank retained its heightened readiness to sell the franc, guarding against renewed geopolitical turmoil in a decision that also left borrowing costs unchanged.

item.title: BHP Raises Potash Mine Costs Again, Takes $2.3 Billion Writedown
item.description: BHP Group said it expects the expansion of its giant Jansen potash mine to cost $6.9 billion, the latest in a series of cost and time overruns for the project in Canada.

item.title: Indonesia Raises Rates Again as Rupiah Defense Continues
item.description: Indonesia’s central bank raised its key interest rate for a third time in about a month, extending its aggressive efforts to stabilize the rupiah and shore up investor confidence.

item.title: Pound Volatility Hits March High Before BOE Meeting, By-Election
item.description: The cost of hedging swings in the value of the pound hit the highest in almost three months as traders await a Bank of England interest-rate decision on Thursday and a by-election that could trigger a bid to oust UK premier Keir Starmer.

item.title: Switzerland Loses Top Competitiveness Ranking to Singapore
item.description: Switzerland lost its position as the world’s most competitive economy to Singapore, slipping to third place in the ranking as high US trade tariffs and a strong Swiss franc hurt investment flows.

item.title: JPMorgan’s Ward Says Falling Oil Can Unleash European Stocks
item.description: It’s time to buy European stocks, according to JPMorgan Asset Management’s Karen Ward.

item.title: China Stock Gauge Heads for Bear Market as Tech Weakness Extends
item.description: A major Chinese stock gauge is on track for a bear market, pressured by weakness in Internet and consumer firms.

item.title: Philippines Hikes Rate Again to Temper War-Driven Inflation
item.description: The Philippine central bank increased its benchmark interest rate for a second consecutive meeting, as it braces for a prolonged battle against inflation despite an interim US-Iran peace deal.

item.title: Traders See 40-Year Yen Low as Next Intervention Battleground
item.description: Yen watchers are bracing for the currency to slide to its weakest level in four decades against the dollar, a move that many see as the next threshold for the authorities to intervene.

item.title: Blackstone’s AirTrunk Seeks $3 Billion Loan for Sydney Project
item.description: AirTrunk Pty., a data center operator backed by Blackstone Inc., is in talks with banks for a A$4.3 billion ($3 billion) loan to back a new project in Australia, people familiar with the matter said, as it continues its debt-fueled expansion across Asia Pacific.

item.title: Indoor Playground Price Rises Add to Costs for British Families
item.description: After recovering from the pandemic, soft-play centers are hiking prices to cushion the blow of soaring costs.

item.title: Tesco Sales Disappoint as Wetter Weather Deters UK Shoppers
item.description: Tesco Plc’s sales rose less than expected in the fiscal first quarter as the UK’s biggest supermarket grapples with weaker consumer sentiment and a tough comparison with last year.

item.title: Anthropic Ban Forces Investor Rethink of Political Risk
item.description: Investors in the ever-hotter AI stock rally must suddenly consider a risk with the potential to be even more damaging than high valuations and big spending: Politics getting in the way.

item.title: UK Jobs Market Slowdown Eases Ahead of Bank of England Decision
item.description: Britain’s labor market has held up better than expected since the Iran war started, according to figures published on Thursday just hours before the Bank of England’s latest interest-rate decision.

item.title: The Iran War May Be Over. Higher Food Prices Aren’t.
item.description: The supply chain of a chocolate croissant exposes how rising costs are rippling through every stage of the food business — with no relief in sight.

item.title: Russia Piles Up Debt as Costs of War in Ukraine Outrun Budget
item.description: President Vladimir Putin frequently touts Russia’s lowest public debt among Group of 20 nations as a key strength of its economy. His war in Ukraine is starting to change that.

item.title: UK Unemployment Falls, Wages Grow Faster Than Expected
Citi Scraps Calls for RBI Hikes as Iran Deal Cools Price Risks
item.description: Economists at Citigroup Inc. dropped their call for two interest-rate hikes by India’s central bank through March next year, saying an interim US-Iran peace deal has reduced the risk of higher oil prices fueling inflation.

item.title: India Taps World Bank, ADB for $2.5 Billion Infrastructure Spend
item.description: India is said to be in talks with multilateral lenders to secure about $2.5 billion in funding from existing credit lines as New Delhi tees up fresh sources of money after the Middle East conflict crimped its ability to step up spending.

item.title: Kingboard’s 570% Rally Is Being Powered by Mainland Investors
item.description: Chinese investors have fueled a surge in shares of newfound AI darling Kingboard Laminates Holdings Ltd., more than doubling their stake in the company to 13% this year, according to the latest stock exchange data.

item.title: Copper Falls After Fed Chairman Warsh Positions as Inflation Hawk
item.description: Copper fell more than 1% to wipe out the gains of earlier this week after US Federal Reserve Chair Kevin Warsh sparked speculation about rate hikes in comments at his debut press conference.

item.title: Mukesh Ambani’s Promise to Investors on Jio IPO Will Be Tested This Week
item.description: The long-running saga over the listing of Reliance Industries Ltd.’s digital unit comes to the fore this week, as investors watch whether its billionaire chairman Mukesh Ambani can walk the talk from last year.

item.title: Circles Sold Phone Spy Tools to Repressive States, Report Says
item.description: A Bulgaria-based company sold controversial surveillance technology to governments in countries with records of repression, enabling authorities to track mobile phones and eavesdrop on private communications, according to documents obtained by Human Rights Watch.

item.title: Europe Bets Industrial AI Can Salvage Its Manufacturing Edge
item.description: Pressure to become more efficient has Europe racing to bring artificial intelligence to the shop floor.

item.title: Czechs Mull First Rate Hike Since 2022 to Curb Price Pressures
item.description: The Czech central bank may raise interest rates for the first time in four years as policymakers weigh domestic inflation pressures against receding risks from global energy prices.

item.title: Europe Tries to Take On China Without Launching a New Trade War
item.description: The Group of Seven industrial nations this week pledged an ambitious target to diversify away from China, adding momentum to the European Union’s own push to counter a growing trade imbalance with the world’s biggest export engine.

item.title: Rio Tinto Resumes Copper Exports From Massive Mongolian Mine
item.description: Rio Tinto Group has resumed exports of copper concentrate from its vast Oyu Tolgoi copper mine in Mongolia following a short protest.

item.title: India Monsoon Starts Almost 40% Short as El Niño Upends Weather
item.description: India’s monsoon has had an unusually weak start and the lackluster rainfall is likely to persist, threatening crops and industrial activity as the El Niño weather phenomenon takes hold.

item.title: Goldman Says Hormuz Oil Flows May Recover to Only 70% After War
item.description: Oil flows going through the Strait of Hormuz may recover to only about 70% of their pre-war level, according to Goldman Sachs Group Inc., which highlighted regional producers leaning on alternative routes.
